source.title: Self-monitoring dysfunction and the positive symptoms of schizophrenia

confidence_rationale: N=40 schizophrenia patients (DSM-III-R) vs N=36 controls. Self-monitoring battery showed patient impairment on two tests, surviving covariate adjustment for IQ, attention, recognition memory. Among patients, self-monitoring performance correlated with severity and extent of positive symptoms. Replication-class evidence for the Frith account; clean covariate adjustment improves the inference. Source quality solid, mapping fidelity high (the paradigms operationalise self-monitoring directly), modality risk moderate (different self-monitoring tasks vs MECH-095's hazard-field counterfactual gap, but the principle is identical).
failure_signatures: If REE produces a working MECH-095 but downstream consumers of MECH-095 output fail to manifest the self-monitoring -> positive-symptom-severity gradient when challenged, the architectural reading would be that MECH-095 is intact but its downstream wiring (to E3 commitment gates, RC-loop checks, etc.) is broken. The Stirling correlation provides a quantitative target for healthy-vs-impaired performance comparison.

mapping.source_claim_statement: In a clinical-vs-control study (N=40 patients DSM-III-R schizophrenia vs N=36 controls), patients showed impaired performance on two self-monitoring tasks. Impairment was preserved after covariate adjustment for IQ, attention, and recognition memory. Self-monitoring performance among patients was correlated with the severity and extent of positive symptoms (delusions, hallucinations, passivity).
mapping.ree_translation: Stirling et al. provide replication-class behavioral evidence for the same self-monitoring breakdown that Blakemore 2000 documented at the perceptual level. For MECH-095 the implication is that the comparator-failure account is well-replicated and survives covariate adjustment for general cognitive function -- i.e. the impairment is specific to self-monitoring rather than a global cognitive deficit. The dose-response gradient (self-monitoring performance correlates with positive-symptom severity) is the kind of quantitative signature MECH-095 should reproduce: parametric variation in MECH-095 competence should produce parametric variation in downstream symptom-analog metrics.
mapping.mapping_caveat: Stirling's tasks are self-monitoring batteries (likely including action-monitoring and verbal-self-monitoring tests; see full text), not the specific tactile attenuation paradigm of Blakemore 2000. The mapping into MECH-095's hazard-field comparator output is principled but indirect. Effect sizes from this 2001 N=40 study are also smaller than would be required for a definitive between-cohort claim today.
mapping.source_context: Schizophrenia patients (N=40, DSM-III-R) compared with non-patient controls (N=36). Self-monitoring battery + cognitive function measures (IQ, attention, recognition memory). Symptom severity rated from clinical interview.

confidence_components.notes: Source quality moderate (Psychopathology, modest sample size by current standards, DSM-III-R era). Mapping fidelity good because the variable measured (self-monitoring performance correlating with positive symptom severity) is structurally what MECH-095 should reproduce. Transfer risk moderate due to task modality difference.
